coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of a novel red-emissive carbon dots powder formulation and its efficiency evaluation in latent fingerprint development using artificial intelligence
coredata.dc:creator: Lighvan, Vahid Ashrafi
Arsalani, Nasser
coredata.dc:description: The development and comparison of latent fingerprints (LFPs) yield crucial insights within the realm of forensic science. This procedure involves two-step aimed at enhancing the visualization of LFPs and conducting digital processing to facilitate their comparison with a database. Herein, red-emissive Nitrogen and Boron codoped carbon dots (N, B-CDred) were synthesized by solvothermal method. The TEM analysis of N, B-CDred provides visual evidence for the successful formation of sub-3 nm carbon dots. The reduced size of N, B-CDred offers advantages in terms of their ability to be effectively incorporated into the pore structure of various matrices. Subsequently, the amalgamation of N, B-CDred with a blend of plaster and corn starch resulted in the formation of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors. The aforementioned phosphors was efficacious in the development of LFPs using the dusting technique, whereby the emission of red fluorescence resulting from the excitation of fluorophore N, B-CDred under green light substantially enhance the visualization of LFPs. By employing an artificial intelligence program to analyze fluorescence images of the developed LFPs, the results demonstrate remarkable match scores exceeding 90 % for the samples, indicating a substantial similarity to the “Control” reference.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Latent fingerprints (LFPs)
N, B-CDred
N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors
Artificial intelligence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of n, b-cdred. b) Size distribution histogram of N, B-CDred.

A) Uv–visible absorption (black line) and pl emission (red line) spectra of n, b-cdred, inset of a) Ethanol solution of N, B-CDred exposed to UV light, b) PL emission in various excitation-wavelengths.
Raman spectrum of the N, B-CDred and it's dependent deconvoluted details.
XP survey spectra of N, B-CDred, along with Relative contents of C, N, O, and B elements.
The deconvoluted details (a) b 1s, (b) C 1s, and (c) N 1s, respectively, (d-f) Relative contents of functional groups.
FT-IR spectra of the N, B-CDred, Plaster & Corn starch mixture, and N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors.

Photographs of the N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors-stained LFPs on (a) glass, (b) plastic, and (c) steel substrates under green light irradiation, respectively. (d) Fluorescence microscope image of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors-stained LFP on a glass sheet.

Digital processing steps for LFP on glass and database, including (a) initial input image, (b) output image. (d) The similarity score of fingerprints across various substrates, along with the average authentication for fingerprints attributed to a single individual.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of a novel red-emissive carbon dots powder formulation and its efficiency evaluation in latent fingerprint development using artificial intelligence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 29-Bahman Blvd, Tabriz, East-Azarbiajan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 29-Bahman Blvd, Tabriz, East-Azarbiajan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development and comparison of latent fingerprints (LFPs) yield crucial insights within the realm of forensic science. This procedure involves two-step aimed at enhancing the visualization of LFPs and conducting digital processing to facilitate their comparison with a database. Herein, red-emissive Nitrogen and Boron codoped carbon dots (N, B-CDred) were synthesized by solvothermal method. The TEM analysis of N, B-CDred provides visual evidence for the successful formation of sub-3 nm carbon dots. The reduced size of N, B-CDred offers advantages in terms of their ability to be effectively incorporated into the pore structure of various matrices. Subsequently, the amalgamation of N, B-CDred with a blend of plaster and corn starch resulted in the formation of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors. The aforementioned phosphors was efficacious in the development of LFPs using the dusting technique, whereby the emission of red fluorescence resulting from the excitation of fluorophore N, B-CDred under green light substantially enhance the visualization of LFPs. By employing an artificial intelligence program to analyze fluorescence images of the developed LFPs, the results demonstrate remarkable match scores exceeding 90 % for the samples, indicating a substantial similarity to the “Control” reference.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For over a century, forensic scientists have relied on the identification and analysis of fingerprints found at crime scenes as crucial evidence for court proceedings [1,2]. Certain ridge patterns are left on solid substrates when fingers come into contact with them due to natural secretions from the skin, such as eccrine sweat and sebum [2]. However, the visual detection of these patterns is challenging due to the poor optical contrast between the ridges and the underlying substrate, which generally precludes observation by the naked eye. Fingerprints that are not readily visible are described as “latent fingerprints” (LFPs) [3,4]. The detection of LFPs, particularly through techniques collectively referred to as LFP development, is of significant scientific interest. The ultimate goal is to accurately capture and record the ridge patterns of developed LFPs using various imaging methods [5–7]. This process involves applying scientific principles and methodologies to enhance the accuracy and reliability of fingerprint analysis in forensic investigations. Furthermore, it is imperative that the developed LFP images be rigorously compared with a comprehensive database of fingerprints belonging to known criminal offenders. This comparison entails utilizing standardized software equipped with algorithms that compute matching scores to rank the similarity between the developed fingerprint and those in the database [8–10]. Employing essential scientific methods ensures the accuracy and reliability of fingerprint analysis results, thereby facilitating the identification and prosecution of criminal perpetrators [11]. Several scientific methods have been developed to enhance the visualization of LFP on solid surfaces. These techniques include powder dusting, cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming, the ninhydrin method, fluorescent imaging using 1,8-diazafluoren-9-one, and vacuum metal deposition, among others. The effectiveness of these methods is based on the chemical or physical reactions that occur between various materials and the residual fat on the surfaces [3,11–15]. Such reactions improve the visualization of LFPs and increase the likelihood of identification. However, employing these techniques requires a high degree of technical skill and knowledge to ensure optimal results in forensic investigations. Among these methods, powder dusting is one of the most widely used techniques for developing LFPs. It involves using a polymer-based powder (e.g., starch or kaolin) to enhance adhesion to fingerprint residue, along with pigments such as carbon black and fuller's earth to improve visual contrast [16]. Despite its popularity, the effectiveness of powder dusting is limited by factors such as low contrast, inadequate selectivity and sensitivity, and the high toxicity levels associated with some chemicals used [17]. Consequently, research is increasingly focused on developing safer and more effective techniques for LFP detection. The potential method of employing fluorescent dye staining has been proposed to improve the visualization of LFPs by amplifying their optical contrast [18]. This approach entails the application of photoluminescent colored powders, such as rhodamine B-dye [19], onto residual fat located on the substrates. High optical contrast can be yielded and visualization of LFPs can be enhanced by this technique under light emission. While this method can yield high optical contrast, challenges remain in achieving clear detection of fingerprint lines, as the powder can cover a large surface area. To ensure fluidity and prevent powder adherence to substrates, it is necessary to disperse dye powders within a substantial amount of carrier powder. When evaluating the efficacy of this technique, these factors must be considered [10]. Additionally, it is believed that background interference can be effectively mitigated by using powders and solutions containing nanoparticles. This belief arises from the smaller size of luminescent nanoparticles compared to both arches and surface terminals, allowing for targeted adhesion to specific regions of interest while minimizing extraneous signals from substrates [10,20]. However, further research is needed to fully understand and measure the advantages of fingerprint analysis using nanoparticle-based techniques. In this context, carbon-based quantum dots are favored due to their non-toxic nature. Specifically, quasi-zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with inherent photoluminescence (PL) properties, commonly referred to as carbon dots (CDs), have been frequently utilized [21]. The CDs offer several advantages, such as high stability under various conditions, superior biocompatibility, and low toxicity [22]. Additionally, they can be synthesized using a variety of simple methods, facilitating large-scale production. The use of CDs in fingerprint analysis shows great promise and warrants further investigation. Despite the advantages of utilizing CDs in fingerprint analysis, several significant challenges must also be addressed. Firstly, most CDs emit blue or green light, and their photoluminescence can be disrupted by the substrate before contrast enhancement occurs. Secondly, CDs cannot be directly used for dusting due to the phenomenon known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which results in a loss of luminescence in the solid state due to particle aggregation [23,24]. ACQ causes luminescence to disappear in the solid state due to particle aggregation [25].These challenges must be addressed in order to effectively utilize CDs for fingerprint detection. Ongoing research aims to address the limitations in the synthesis and application of CDs, with the goal of developing improved techniques that enhance the sensitivity and visualization of LFPs [10,26].

The unique properties of CDs, including their facile synthesis methods [27], stable photoluminescence [28], multifunctionality [29], and biocompatibility [30], have garnered the interest of researchers. These advantages make CDs an attractive research subject for various fields, including gene transmission [31], drug carriers [32,33], sensing [34], therapeutics [35], and photocatalysis [36].

In recent years, a growing body of literature has reported successful synthesis of blue- and green-emitting CDs that exhibit high levels of photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). However, the synthesis of red-emitting CDs has been comparatively limited [37,38]. It has been observed that the imaging quality of CDs can be improved with red emission, which can also neutralize substrate interference [10,26,39].

The development of LFPs using the luminescent solutions relies on the coffee-ring effect. For this effect to be observed in LFP development, the luminescent properties of the sprayed solution must be retained after drying within the boundary region of the ridges [26,39,40]. Furthermore, these methods are often restricted by costly preparation routes, potential fingerprint degradation, and susceptibility to instability in practical environments [41].

In this study, we have introduced a facile two-step methodology for the preparation of red-emissive phosphors with the purpose of advancing the development of LFPs. The present approach entails the implementation of solvothermal synthesis to produce red-emissive N, B-codoped CD (N, B-CDred). Subsequently, the N, B-CDred are incorporated into a composite mixture comprising plaster and corn starch. We utilized the phosphors prepared to develop LFPs on different substrates using the powder-dusting technique. Under the stimulation of green light irradiation, the utilization of N, B-CDred enhances the visualization of LFPs by generating red fluorescence, while simultaneously there is minimum interference from background fluorescence. In this research endeavor, we have developed a digital processing algorithm that is capable of identifying LFPs in an objective manner by extracting their characteristic features. Subsequently, the program accurately compares the extracted features with a “database” and provides matching scores. It was the developed LFP from the glass substrate that achieved the highest match rate to the “database”, 98.90 %.
Neutral Red (NR) (99.0 %), boric acid (BA) (99.99 %), and diethylene glycol (99.99 %) were acquired from Merk.

A solution containing of Neutral Red (1.080 g), boric acid (0.12 g), and 27.0 mL diethylene glycol underwent magnetic stirring for 10 min, yielding a transparent red solution. The resulting solution was then sealed within an autoclave that was lined with poly(tetrafluoroethylene). Subsequently, the sealed solution was subjected to a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 6 h. Upon reaching ambient temperature, the resulting product underwent dialysis (using a 3.5 kDa molecular weight cut-off membrane) against ethanol for a duration of 24 h. Subsequently, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried and kept for future use. The product was denoted as N, B-CDred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture was prepared through the combination of 5.0 ml of N, B-CDred ethanol solution, possessing a concentration of 5.0 (mg/ml), with 500 mg of corn starch and 500 mg of plaster. The mixture was then subjected to concurrent stirring and heating until it underwent desiccation and formed a powdered state. The product was denoted as N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch.

This study was conducted with the objective of systematically collecting and documenting fingerprints from an individual for analytical purposes. The participant adhered to a consistent hand hygiene regimen, routinely cleansing their hands with soap and water. Subsequent to the completion of the hygiene protocol, the individual intentionally transferred their fingerprints onto glass, plastic, and steel surfaces by tactile interaction with their facial area and pressing their index finger onto the designated surfaces. The latent fingerprints (LFPs) were then developed utilizing the powder dusting technique. A meticulous procedure was employed, involving the uniform application of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch powder onto the latent fingerprints using a fine feather brush. Any surplus powder was carefully removed using a brush to ensure clarity of the developed patterns. Subsequently, the developed fingerprints were subjected to illumination with a green light source. The resulting fluorescent patterns were captured using a mobile phone camera equipped with a magenta light filter. This filtering selectively blocked the green light, enhancing the visibility of the red fluorescence in the captured images.

The morphology of the N, B-CDred was examined through the utilization of transmission electron microscopy (TEM). To accomplish this, a trace quantity of the purified N, B-CDred suspension was meticulously placed onto a TEM carbon grid after separating it from the bulk using ultrasonic treatment. Fig. 1 a displays a TEM image related to the N, B-CDred that provides supporting evidence for the successful synthesis of spherical nanoparticles. Fig. 1b depicts the Gaussian fitting size distribution curves, which correspond to the Fig. 1a. A careful analysis of the aforementioned curve reveals a pronounced homogeneity in the distribution of the N, B-CDred being studied, implying their monodisperse characteristics. Moreover, the average size of N, B-CDred was estimated to be 2.74 nm.

Fig. 2 a presents the optical characterization findings of N, B-CDred. The purified N, B-CDred exhibits a prominent absorption band at 450 nm, which is commonly associated with the n → π* transition of conjugated CO and CN functional groups. Furthermore, a strong absorption peak at 250 nm, ascribed to the π → π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, was also observed. The (quantum yield) QY of N, B-CDred was investigated by comparing it with Rhodamine-6G (R-6G) as the reference dye using the following formula. (According to previous researches, the QY of R-6G in Ethanol was reported to be 0.95).
Φ s
=
Φ r
×
I s
I r
×
A
r
A s
×
η s
η r
2
where, Φ, I, A, and η are assigned to QY, integrated PL intensity, absorbance values, and refractive index, respectively. The subscript r and s refer R-6G and N, B-CDred, respectively. Upon performing calculations, it was ascertained that the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of N, B-CDred has exceeded the threshold of 8.0 %. Photoluminescence spectroscopy was utilized as a rigorous technique for systematically examining the changes in emitted light from an ethanolic medium containing N, B-CDred. The study involved precise modulation of the excitation wavelength to analyze the variation in photoluminescence characteristics. In inset of Fig. 2a, there is an image of an ethanolic solution of purified N, B-CDred illuminated under UV light. Based on the observations depicted in Fig. 2b, it is evident that the light emission intensity does not exhibit correlation with the variation in excitation wavelength. Nevertheless, the maximum emission wavelength and full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission peaks show consistency despite changes in the excitation wavelength. Consequently, it can be asserted with confidence that the N, B-CDred exhibit excitation-independent luminescence. Fig. S1 displays the results obtained from the examination of carbon dots and neutral red dissolved within acetone, diethylene glycol, and ethanol solvents, while subject to UV light. The discernible difference in the emission wavelength provides additional evidence substantiating the accomplishment of effective carbon dots synthesis.

The Raman spectroscopy was conducted in order to ascertain the degree of crystallinity possessed by the synthesized nanostructure (Fig. 3 ). The process of deconvoluting the Raman spectrum within the range of 1050–1700 cm−1 revealed two distinct peaks located at 1358 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1, which can be attributed to the A1g breathing mode and E2g phonon mode, respectively. The A1g breathing mode is an inherent feature observed in amorphous carbon, leading to the formation of a distinctive spectral band known as the “D band.” Conversely, the E2g phonon mode is typically linked to graphitic carbon and leads to the emergence of a spectral band known as the “G band.” The defect intensity (ID/IG) of N, B-CDred was determined to be 2.55, surpassing the ratio observed in undoped graphene quantum dots (1.01). Considering that defect intensity is indicative of the quantity of structural defects, it can be inferred that doping amplifies the edge roughness and defects in graphene sheets, thereby resulting in an increased ID/IG ratio. After performing deconvolution on the Raman spectrum analysis, two supplementary peaks emerged at 1189 cm−1 and 1501 cm−1, correspondingly designated as the D* and D** band. The hypotheses are grounded in the understanding that the origin of the D* band lies in the graphitic lattice, which was comprised a combination of sp2 and sp3 carbon bonding, whereas the D** band originates from trans-polyacetylene present in the grain boundaries [42,43].

The structural characteristics of N, B-CDred were investigated using the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique, as depicted in Fig. S2. The XRD analysis of N, B-CDred revealed a distinct, sharp peak at 2ϴ = 26.0°, corresponding to a specific d-spacing of 0.34 nm along the (002) crystallographic direction.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was employed to investigate the surface functional groups of the N, B-CDred, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The XPS spectrum provided confirmation that N, B-CDred consists of nitrogen, boron, carbon, and oxygen elements, with relative contents of 10.39 %, 6.94 %, 75.94 %, and 6.73 % respectively. Analyzing high-resolution XP spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and B1s allowed for a more comprehensive perception of the surface functional groups. The deconvolution of the high-resolution XP C1s spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 5 b., revealed the presence of multiple carbon binding sites at specific energy levels: 283.5, 284.7, 285.6, and 287.0 eV. The mentioned energy levels correspond to different functional groups, namely COH, CC, CN, and CN, respectively [44,45]. The graphitization process of the carbon precursor, Neutral red, is corroborated by empirical observations revealing that the CC bonds contribute to over 30 % of the total carbon content (Fig. 5e). Through the process of deconvoluting XP N1s, it was deduced that the nitrogen content of the N, B-CDred encompasses CNC, Pyridinic-, Pyrrolic-, and Quaternary-N species, with corresponding bond energies of 398.9, 397.8, 400.1, and 402.1 eV, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 5c [46–48]. Fig. 5a demonstrates the deconvolution process applied to a high-resolution XPS short scan of B1s, revealing the presence of three distinct B-binding sites at energy values of 188.5 eV (BC), 190.3 eV (BN), and 191.7 eV (BN) [49–53]. One potential outcome derived from deconvoluting the peak associated with boron is the formation of BN bonds, signifying the condensation reaction between boric acid, amino, pyrrolic-N of Neutral red that ultimately facilitated the creation of graphite frameworks (Fig. S3). The relative proportions of the mentioned functional groups are depicted in the Fig. 5d.

In order to conduct a more comprehensive analysis of the functional groups, the Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded for the mixture of N, B-CDred, plaster& corn starch mixture, as well as for the N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors Fig. 6 . In the FT-IR spectrum of N, B-CDred, absorption bands related to out-of-plane BN stretching, CN stretching, in-plane BN stretching, CC (in ring) stretching, CO/CN stretching, CH stretching and OH/NH stretching were observed at 808, 1194, 1322, 1442& 1497, 1626, 3181 and 3200–3500 cm−1, respectively. Both plaster& corn starch mixture and N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors exhibited completely identical absorption bands at 601& 662& 1092, 1153, 765& 859& 928, 1625, 2930, and 3400–3600 cm−1 which corresponded to Sulfate vibrations from plaster, COC stretching, COC pyranose ring of glucose, Water molecular vibrations in plaster, CH stretching, OH stretching respectively [54].

In a previous study of similar nature [10], starch was employed as a matrix for the incorporation of carbon dots exhibiting red light emission. However, empirical evidence obtained through iterative experimentation indicated that starch exhibited limited adhesion as a carrier fluid across diverse surface types. Consequently, the present investigation aimed to introduce a novel matrix as a carrier fluid to accommodate the dispersion and transport of carbon dots. Based on the scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis of the N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors (Fig. S4) it is evident that the adhesive properties of fine plaster particles to the porous surface of starch grains result in the better adherence of starch grains to finger fatty residues.

Following the reported optimal mass ratio of 1:40 [10], N, B-CDred was combined with a mixture of corn starch and plaster. The resulting N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors was evaluated for investigation their efficiency in developing LFPs on glass, plastic, and steel substrates. The results presented in Fig. 7 a-c demonstrate the success in developing and identifying LFPs on the substrates mentioned earlier. The high resolution of the protrusion patterns in all samples can be attributed to the high contrast produced by the fluorescent signal, as evident from the images presented. The feature points that determine the uniqueness of a fingerprint are clearly discernible due to the high resolution of the image. The feature points associated with the developed LFP on the glass substrate were identified and are illustrated in Fig. 7a. The feature points comprise island, scar, bifurcation, eye, crossover, core, termination, and ridge divergence. The fluorescence microscope image of LFP developed on a glass substrate using N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors clearly depicts fluorescent phosphors particles that adhere to the sweat and fat residues of the index finger, resulting in a pattern with high contrast (Fig. 7d). Fig. S5 illustrates the fluorescence microscopy image of N, B-CDred@ plaster-corn starch phosphors after being stored under ambient conditions for over 10 months. The observed similarities with the image in Fig. 7d suggest the remarkable stability of the N, B-CDred@ plaster-corn starch phosphors over this duration. Additionally, Fig. S6 displays the fluorescence microscope image of N, B-CDred@ corn starch phosphors, which appears qualitatively similar to Fig. 7d.

Utilizing artificial intelligence in the analysis of LFPs images through digital processing represents a swift and effective method. The Python script employs the Hessian matrix, which is a square matrix containing second-order partial derivatives of a scalar function, to identify ridges by pinpointing areas exhibiting noteworthy intensity fluctuations, as outlined in Eq. (1) [55,56].
(1)
H
( x , y )
=
D
xx
D
xy
D
xy
D
yy

The image processing pipeline incorporates algorithms for image sharpening, grayscale conversion, and histogram equalization. Image sharpening entails convolving the image with a sharpening kernel to enhance edges and intricate features. Subsequently, the sharpened image undergoes a conversion to grayscale to facilitate subsequent processing and analysis. Histogram equalization enhances image contrast by redistributing intensity values, a process involving the calculation of the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the image's histogram and the re-mapping of intensity values. Furthermore, leveraging the premise that the Human Visual System (HVS) is adept at extracting structural details, the Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) algorithm gauges similarity based on three components: luminance l (x, y), contrast c (x, y), and structure s (x, y) [57], using Eq. (2):
(2)
l
( x , y )
=
2
μ x
μ y
+
C 1
μ
x
2
+
μ
y
2
+
C 1
c
( x , y )
=
2
σ x
σ y
+
C 2
σ
x
2
+
σ
y
2
+
C 2
s
( x , y )
=
σ
xy
+
C 3
σ x
σ y
+
C 3

The SSIM algorithm integrates small constants into its framework: C1 = (K1L)2, C2 = (K2L)2, C3 = 0.5C2, where L signifies the dynamic range of pixel values, and K1, K2 represent scalar constants typically significantly less than 1. These constants play a crucial role in maintaining stability, particularly when the denominator tends towards zero. The unified representation of the SSIM algorithm is delineated as, incorporating Eq. (3):
(3)
SSIM ( x , y ) = [ l ( x , y ) ] [ c ( x , y ) ] [ s ( x , y ) ]
=
2
μ x
μ y
+
C 1
2
σ
xy
+
C 2
μ
x
2
+
μ
y
2
+
C 1
σ
x
2
+
σ
y
2
+
C 2

The image possessing the maximum similarity score, which varies between −1 and 1 with 1 the denoting images that are identical, is pinpointed as the optimal match. Subsequently, this most closely matched image is exhibited alongside an annotation containing the filename and similarity score. Subsequent to this, the software sequentially processes each image within a designated folder, applies preprocessing steps, and employs the SSIM algorithm to compare them against the input image. The image exhibiting the highest similarity score is recognized as the most suitable match and is presented with an annotation incorporating both the file name and similarity score.

The computer program yielded matching scores of 98.90 %, 97.57 %, and 97.56 % for LFPs on glass, plastic, and steel substrate, respectively. The Fig. 8 a-c displays the input, processed, and output images associated with both the control sample and the LFP obtained from the glass surface. According to the column chart depicted in Fig. 8d the authentication rate of an individual participant in this study reached an impressive 98.01 %.

In this research, the confirmation of novel carbon dots' synthesis is attained utilizing TEM image and the distinctive variation in emission wavelength between different carbon dot solutions and neutral red when subjected to UV light.

Moreover, the favorable attributes of this study in comparison to other analogous investigations can be delineated as follows: 1) The using of plaster has led to augmented adhesive properties of corn starch, serving as a carrier fluid.
2) The findings pertaining to the percentage of similarity between the developed fingerprints and the control sample have been enhanced.
3) The production of carbon dots with a size below 3 nm, which facilitates the rapid absorption of red emissive carbon dots into the corn starch-plaster matrix.
4) A comparative analysis is conducted between the developed fingerprint and the fingerprint captured through conventional methods utilizing an ink pad.

Using a mild hydrothermal technique and appropriate solvent selection, we successfully synthesized carbon quantum dots with uniform size, measuring less than 3 nm. Subsequently, a novel powder formulation for the development LFPs through the dusting technique was devised by amalgamating the previously mentioned carbon quantum dots with a blend of plaster and corn starch. Our research offers distinct advantages over previous studies concerning the detection of fluorescent fingerprints. To begin, our methodology showcases a facile and expeditious technique for incorporating carbon dots into the plaster and corn starch matrix. This approach effectively circumvents the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) process typically encountered in the solid phase. Second, the utilization of synthetic carbon dots with red fluorescence further enhances the efficacy of visualizing latent fingerprints by effectively mitigating background fluorescence interference. Thirdly, the facile preparation and utilization of N, B-CDred @ plaster-corn starch phosphors exhibit characteristics of simplicity, portability, rapidity, safety, and superiority over conventional methodologies. Lastly, the implementation of a digital processing algorithm facilitates the objective and quantitative evaluation of the resemblance between the developed LFPs and control, enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the determination. The observation of an excellent match score of 98.90 % in our study provides compelling evidence that our material and methodology represent a viable and promising option for practical applications.
Vahid Ashrafi Lighvan: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis. Nasser Arsalani: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111596.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of fingerprints by the ninhydrin reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Oden, B.V.O.N. HOFSTEN, Detection of fingerprints by the ninhydrin reaction, Nature. 173 (1954) 449–450.
P. Hazarika, D.A. Russell, Advances in fingerprint analysis, Angew. Chemie Int. Ed. 51 (2012) 3524–3531.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Powder method for detecting latent fingerprints: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.S. Sodhi, J. Kaur, Powder method for detecting latent fingerprints: a review, Forensic Sci. Int. 120 (2001) 172–176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid imaging of latent fingerprints using biocompatible fluorescent silica nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-J. Kim, H.-S. Jung, J. Lim, S.-J. Ryu, J.-K. Lee, Rapid imaging of latent fingerprints using biocompatible fluorescent silica nanoparticles, Langmuir. 32 (2016) 8077–8083.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A critical review of fundamentals and applications of electrochemical development and imaging of latent fingerprints

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Yuan, M. Li, M. Wang, H. Cao, T. Lin, A critical review of fundamentals and applications of electrochemical development and imaging of latent fingerprints, Electrochim. Acta. 390 (2021) 138798.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The selective deprotonation of carbon quantum dots for fluorescence detection of phosphate and visualization of latent fingerprints

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, Q. Zhang, Y. Han, H. Li, S. Sun, Y. Xu, The selective deprotonation of carbon quantum dots for fluorescence detection of phosphate and visualization of latent fingerprints, Nanoscale. 13 (2021) 13057–13064.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emitting quantum dot nanohybrid for imaging of latent fingerprints: simultaneous identification of individuals and traffic light-type visualization of TNT

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wu, C. Xu, X. Hou, J.-J. Xu, H.-Y. Chen, Dual-emitting quantum dot nanohybrid for imaging of latent fingerprints: simultaneous identification of individuals and traffic light-type visualization of TNT, Chem. Sci. 6 (2015) 4445–4450.
